1. What is Caching?¶

Caching means storing frequently accessed data in fast storage, usually memory, so the system does not repeatedly hit the database.

Application
   ↓
Cache
   ↓ cache miss
Database

Main goal:

Reduce database load
Improve latency
Serve repeated reads faster

Caching is mostly used for read-heavy systems.

⬆ Back to Index

2. Why Use Caching?¶

Database reads may take tens of milliseconds.

Cache reads may take around one millisecond or less.

Example:

Postgres read → ~50 ms
Redis read    → ~1 ms

Caching helps when:

  • Database CPU is high
  • Same data is read repeatedly
  • Query is expensive
  • Low latency is required
  • Read traffic is much higher than write traffic

Tradeoff:

Faster reads
But stale data and invalidation become harder

⬆ Back to Index

3. Where to Cache?¶

Cache Layer Example Best For
External cache Redis, Memcached App/database read scaling
CDN cache CloudFront, Cloudflare, Fastly Static/public content
Client-side cache Browser/mobile cache Offline/repeated client reads
In-process cache App memory Small hot values/config

Interview default:

Start with Redis/Memcached external cache.
Mention CDN for static media.
Mention in-process cache only as optimization.

⬆ Back to Index

4. External Cache: Redis / Memcached¶

External cache is the most common system design answer.

Architecture:

Application
   ↓
Redis / Memcached
   ↓ cache miss
Database

Use for:

  • User profiles
  • Product details
  • Trending feeds
  • Session data
  • Video metadata
  • Frequently read objects

Pros:

  • Shared by all app servers
  • Very fast
  • Reduces DB load
  • Supports TTL and eviction

Cons:

  • Network call required
  • Cache can fail
  • Data can become stale

⬆ Back to Index

5. CDN Cache¶

A CDN caches content close to users globally.

User
 ↓
Nearest CDN Edge
 ↓ cache miss
Origin Server / Blob Storage

Best for:

  • Images
  • Videos
  • Static files
  • Public pages
  • Thumbnails
  • Media delivery

Use CDN when:

  • Content is public or shareable
  • Users are global
  • Static/media content is served at scale

⬆ Back to Index

6. Client-Side Cache¶

Client-side cache stores data on the user's device.

Examples:

  • Browser HTTP cache
  • localStorage
  • Mobile app local cache
  • Offline app storage

Use for:

  • Offline access
  • Previously loaded content
  • Images/static assets
  • App state

Problem:

Backend has less control
Invalidation is hard
Data may become stale

⬆ Back to Index

7. In-Process Cache¶

In-process cache stores data inside application server memory.

Good for:

  • Feature flags
  • Config values
  • Small reference data
  • Extremely hot keys
  • Rate limiting counters
  • Small precomputed values

Pros:

Fastest cache
No network call

Cons:

Not shared across servers
Hard invalidation
Limited memory

⬆ Back to Index

8. Cache-Aside / Lazy Loading¶

Most common caching pattern.

1. App checks cache
2. Cache hit → return data
3. Cache miss → query DB
4. Store result in cache
5. Return data

Use by default in interviews.

Pros:

  • Simple
  • Cache only stores data actually used
  • Works well with Redis/Memcached

Cons:

  • First request after miss is slower
  • Need invalidation strategy

⬆ Back to Index

9. Write-Through Cache¶

Application writes to cache.

Cache synchronously writes to database.

App
 ↓ write
Cache
 ↓ write
Database

Pros:

  • Reads are fresh
  • Cache and DB updated together

Cons:

  • Slower writes
  • Cache pollution
  • Dual-write failure risk

Use when:

Reads must return fresh data
Writes can be slightly slower

⬆ Back to Index

10. Write-Behind / Write-Back Cache¶

Application writes to cache first.

Cache writes to database asynchronously later.

App
 ↓ write
Cache
 ↓ async flush
Database

Pros:

  • Very fast writes
  • High throughput

Cons:

  • Data loss possible if cache crashes before flush
  • Eventual consistency

Use for:

  • Metrics
  • Analytics
  • Logs
  • High-volume non-critical writes

⬆ Back to Index

11. Read-Through Cache¶

Cache fetches from DB on miss.

App
 ↓
Cache
 ↓ cache miss
Database

Common in:

  • CDN caching
  • Specialized cache libraries

Interview note:

Use cache-aside for Redis.
Use read-through when discussing CDN/proxy-like cache.

⬆ Back to Index

12. Cache Eviction Policies¶

Policy Meaning Use Case
LRU Remove least recently used Good default
LFU Remove least frequently used Consistently popular items
FIFO Remove oldest inserted item Simple systems
TTL Expire after time Freshness control

Important:

TTL is usually combined with LRU/LFU.

Example:

User profile TTL = 10 minutes
Trending feed TTL = 60 seconds

⬆ Back to Index

13. Cache Stampede / Thundering Herd¶

Problem:

Popular cache key expires
↓
Thousands of requests miss together
↓
All hit DB
↓
DB overload

Solutions:

  • Request coalescing / single flight
  • Cache warming
  • Probabilistic early refresh
  • Background refresh
  • Serve stale while refreshing

Best answer:

Only one request rebuilds the cache.
Others wait or get stale cached value.

⬆ Back to Index

14. Cache Consistency¶

Problem:

Database has new value
Cache still has old value

Solutions:

  • Invalidate cache on writes
  • Use short TTL
  • Accept eventual consistency
  • Use versioned cache keys
  • Read from source of truth when strong consistency is needed

Example:

Update DB
Delete cache key
Next read reloads fresh data

⬆ Back to Index

15. Hot Keys in Cache¶

Hot key = one cache key gets huge traffic.

Example:

user:taylorswift

Solutions:

  • Replicate hot key
  • Use local in-process cache
  • Rate limit abusive traffic
  • Split/salt hot key if possible

Important:

Caching solves repeated DB reads,
but hot keys can still overload the cache layer.

⬆ Back to Index

16. Cache Failure Handling¶

Problem:

Redis goes down
↓
All requests fall back to DB
↓
DB gets overloaded

Solutions:

  • Circuit breaker
  • Rate limiting
  • Fallback to stale data
  • Small in-process cache
  • Graceful degradation
  • Protect DB from stampede

Interview line:

If cache fails, I do not want all traffic to crush the database.

⬆ Back to Index

17. Caching Interview Answer¶

I use caching when the system has high read traffic, repeated expensive queries, or strict latency requirements. I usually start with Redis using the cache-aside pattern: the application checks cache first, falls back to the database on a miss, stores the result with a TTL, and returns it. I cache data that is read often and changes infrequently, such as user profiles, product details, video metadata, or trending feeds. For static media, I use a CDN. I also handle cache invalidation on writes, choose an eviction policy like LRU with TTL, and protect against cache stampede, hot keys, and cache failures using request coalescing, cache warming, hot-key replication, circuit breakers, and graceful degradation.

⬆ Back to Index

18. What is Sharding?¶

Sharding means splitting data across multiple database machines when one database can no longer handle storage, reads, or writes.

Shard 1 → Users 1–1M
Shard 2 → Users 1M–2M
Shard 3 → Users 2M–3M

Each shard has its own:

  • CPU
  • Memory
  • Storage
  • Connection pool

Goal:

No single machine stores all data or handles all traffic.

⬆ Back to Index

19. Partitioning vs Sharding¶

Concept Meaning
Partitioning Split data inside one database
Sharding Split data across multiple databases/machines

Simple difference:

Partitioning = one machine
Sharding = many machines

⬆ Back to Index

20. Shard Key¶

Shard key = field used to decide where data lives.

Example:

Shard by user_id
Distribute using hash(user_id)

Ask:

  • Does it have many unique values?
  • Does it distribute data evenly?
  • Do common queries use this key?
  • Will it avoid hot shards?
  • Will it keep related data together?

⬆ Back to Index

21. Good vs Bad Shard Key¶

Good shard key properties:

Property Meaning
High cardinality Many unique values
Even distribution Data spreads evenly
Query alignment Common queries hit one shard

Good examples:

user_id
order_id
post_id
tenant_id

Bad examples:

is_premium
country
created_at

Why bad?

is_premium → only true/false
country → some countries overloaded
created_at → latest shard gets all new writes

⬆ Back to Index

22. Range-Based Sharding¶

Range sharding groups records by continuous ranges.

Shard 1 → user_id 1–1M
Shard 2 → user_id 1M–2M
Shard 3 → user_id 2M–3M

Pros:

  • Simple
  • Good for range queries

Cons:

  • Can create hot shards
  • New writes may go to latest range

Good for:

  • Multi-tenant systems
  • Range queries
  • Data naturally grouped by ranges

⬆ Back to Index

23. Hash-Based Sharding¶

Hash sharding uses a hash function.

shard = hash(user_id) % number_of_shards

Pros:

  • Even distribution
  • Common default strategy
  • Good for high-cardinality keys

Cons:

  • Adding/removing shards causes data movement
  • Range queries are harder

Better version:

Consistent hashing

⬆ Back to Index

24. Directory-Based Sharding¶

Directory sharding uses a lookup table.

User 15  → Shard 1
User 87  → Shard 4
User 204 → Shard 2

Pros:

  • Very flexible
  • Can move hot users to dedicated shards
  • Supports custom placement logic

Cons:

  • Extra lookup on every request
  • Directory service becomes critical dependency
  • Adds latency
  • More operational complexity

⬆ Back to Index

25. Hot Spots in Sharding¶

Hot spot = one shard receives much more traffic than others.

Example:

Taylor Swift profile/post gets 1000x traffic

Solutions:

  • Move hot key to dedicated shard
  • Use compound shard key
  • Split hot keys
  • Dynamic shard splitting
  • Cache popular data

Important:

Hashing spreads keys evenly.
It does not guarantee traffic is evenly distributed.

⬆ Back to Index

26. Cross-Shard Queries¶

Problem:

Query needs data from many shards

Example:

Top 10 popular posts globally

Solutions:

  • Cache results
  • Precompute using background job
  • Denormalize data
  • Accept only for rare admin queries

Interview warning:

If a common query hits all shards, rethink the shard key or data model.

⬆ Back to Index

27. Cross-Shard Transactions¶

Problem:

User account on Shard 1
Transaction record on Shard 2

Single DB transaction no longer works.

Solutions:

Approach When
Avoid cross-shard transaction Best option
Keep related data together Shard by user_id/tenant_id
Saga pattern Multi-step eventual consistency
2PC Strong consistency but slow/fragile

Best design:

Choose shard key so common transactions stay on one shard.

⬆ Back to Index

28. Modern Databases and Sharding¶

Many systems handle sharding internally.

Database Sharding Behavior
Cassandra Partition key + virtual nodes
DynamoDB Partition key + internal partitions
MongoDB Shard key + chunk balancing
Vitess Sharding layer for MySQL
Citus Sharding layer for PostgreSQL
Spanner Distributed SQL with built-in distribution

Interview examples:

Use DynamoDB with user_id as partition key
Use Vitess to shard MySQL by user_id

⬆ Back to Index

29. Sharding Interview Answer¶

Sharding is splitting data across multiple database machines when a single database can no longer handle storage, read throughput, or write throughput. I would first prove sharding is needed through capacity estimates. Then I would choose a shard key with high cardinality, even distribution, and alignment with query patterns, such as user_id for user-centric systems. I would usually use hash-based sharding with consistent hashing to distribute data evenly and reduce data movement during resharding. I would also address tradeoffs like hot spots, cross-shard queries, cross-shard transactions, and rebalancing.

⬆ Back to Index

30. What Problem Does Consistent Hashing Solve?¶

Consistent hashing distributes keys across servers while minimizing data movement when servers are added or removed.

Used for:

  • Distributed databases
  • Distributed caches
  • CDNs
  • Message brokers
  • Sharded systems

Main question:

How do we decide which server owns which key?

And:

How do we avoid moving almost all data when servers change?

⬆ Back to Index

31. Simple Modulo Hashing¶

Basic approach:

database_id = hash(event_id) % number_of_databases

Example with 3 databases:

Event 1234 → hash(1234) % 3 → DB1
Event 5678 → hash(5678) % 3 → DB0
Event 9012 → hash(9012) % 3 → DB2

This is simple when the number of databases does not change.

⬆ Back to Index

32. Problem with Modulo Hashing¶

If databases change from 3 to 4:

hash(event_id) % 3

becomes:

hash(event_id) % 4

Problem:

Most keys now map to different databases

Result:

  • Massive data movement
  • High DB load
  • Slow responses
  • Rebalancing pain

⬆ Back to Index

33. Consistent Hashing / Hash Ring¶

Consistent hashing places both servers and keys on a circular space called a hash ring.

0 ───── 25 ───── 50 ───── 75 ───── 100
DB1     DB2      DB3      DB4

To place a key:

1. Hash the key
2. Find position on ring
3. Move clockwise
4. Store on first server found

Benefit:

When server count changes, only nearby keys move.

⬆ Back to Index

34. Adding and Removing Nodes¶

Adding a node:

Only keys in the affected ring range move.
Everything else stays where it is.

Removing a node:

Only data from the removed node moves.
That data goes to the next clockwise node.

Benefit:

Failure or scaling event affects only a bounded subset of keys.

⬆ Back to Index

35. Virtual Nodes¶

Problem without virtual nodes:

If one DB fails, its load may move mostly to one neighbor

Solution:

Use virtual nodes

Instead of placing each database once on the ring, place it multiple times.

DB1-v1
DB1-v2
DB1-v3

DB2-v1
DB2-v2
DB2-v3

Benefits:

  • Better load distribution
  • Smoother node addition
  • Smoother node removal

⬆ Back to Index

36. Consistent Hashing vs Hot Spots¶

Consistent hashing distributes keys, not traffic.

Problem:

Taylor Swift concert gets 100x more traffic

Solutions:

Problem Solution
Popular key gets too many reads Read replicas
One key too hot Key salting
Node overloaded Adaptive rebalancing

Important distinction:

Virtual nodes solve uneven key distribution.
Replication/salting solve uneven traffic distribution.

⬆ Back to Index

37. Real-World Usage¶

Used in or related to:

System Usage
Cassandra Distributes data across ring
DynamoDB Partition placement concepts
CDNs Decide edge/cache placement
Distributed caches Map keys to cache nodes
Distributed databases Sharding/partitioning

Redis Cluster uses fixed hash slots instead of classic consistent hashing:

16,384 slots

⬆ Back to Index

38. Consistent Hashing Interview Answer¶

Consistent hashing is used to distribute keys across servers while minimizing data movement when servers are added or removed. Instead of using hash(key) % number_of_servers, we place servers and keys on a hash ring. A key is assigned to the first server clockwise from its hash position. When a server is added or removed, only nearby keys move, not the whole dataset. Virtual nodes improve load balancing, while replication and key salting help handle failures and hot keys.

⬆ Back to Index

39. Why Specialized Data Structures?¶

Some systems process massive datasets where normal exact structures become too expensive.

Examples:

  • Trillions of IDs
  • Billions of events
  • Massive URL crawl sets
  • Huge analytics streams
  • High-cardinality metrics
  • Real-time percentiles

Specialized data structures help when you can accept approximation.

Important warning:

Do not over-engineer.
If a normal hash table works, use it.

These are useful when:

  • Data is huge
  • Memory is limited
  • Approximation is acceptable
  • Exact answers are too expensive

⬆ Back to Index

40. Bloom Filter¶

A Bloom Filter is a probabilistic data structure used for membership checks.

It answers:

Item is definitely not present
OR
Item is probably present

It can have:

  • False positives
  • No false negatives

Meaning:

If Bloom filter says NO → definitely not present
If Bloom filter says YES → maybe present

It is more memory efficient than a hash set but less exact.

⬆ Back to Index

41. Bloom Filter: How It Works¶

Basic idea:

Bit array + multiple hash functions

Insert:

  1. Hash item using k hash functions
  2. Set corresponding bits to 1

Lookup:

  1. Hash item using same k hash functions
  2. Check all corresponding bits
  3. If any bit is 0 → definitely not present
  4. If all bits are 1 → probably present

Example:

item = URL
hash1(URL) → bit 10
hash2(URL) → bit 53
hash3(URL) → bit 91
Set bits 10, 53, 91

As more bits are set, false positive rate increases.

⬆ Back to Index

42. Bloom Filter: Use Cases and Pitfalls¶

Use Bloom Filter when:

  • You need membership checks (insta id)
  • You are memory constrained
  • False positives are acceptable

Good use cases:

  • Web crawler visited URL check
  • Avoid checking cache when key is definitely absent
  • Deduplication with tolerance for false positives
  • Database/storage engine existence checks

Pitfalls:

  • Standard Bloom filters do not support deletion
  • False positives must be acceptable
  • Needs proper sizing
  • Not useful if exact membership is required
  • Can become saturated over time

Interview line:

Bloom filter is useful when I need space-efficient membership checks and can tolerate false positives.

⬆ Back to Index

43. Count-Min Sketch¶

Count-Min Sketch estimates how many times an item appeared in a stream.

It is useful for approximate frequency counting.

It answers:

Approximately how many times did user see each add (may be 20 t adds 10 m users)? Store 20 trillion counters in 16KB with Count Min Sketch

Properties:

  • Uses bounded memory
  • Gives approximate counts
  • Count is usually an upper bound
  • Good for huge streams

Use when exact per-key counters are too expensive.

⬆ Back to Index

44. Count-Min Sketch: How It Works¶

Basic idea:

2D array of counters + multiple hash functions

Insert/increment:

  1. Hash item using multiple hash functions
  2. Increment one counter per row

Query:

  1. Hash item using same hash functions
  2. Read corresponding counters
  3. Return the minimum counter

Why minimum?

Collisions only increase counts.
Taking the minimum reduces overestimation.

Width controls collision probability. Depth controls confidence.

⬆ Back to Index

45. Count-Min Sketch: Use Cases and Pitfalls¶

Use Count-Min Sketch when:

  • You need approximate counts
  • Data stream is huge
  • Memory is limited
  • Approximation is acceptable

Good use cases:

  • Top K videos/views
  • Popular cache keys
  • LFU-like cache analysis
  • Count page views/events
  • Detect heavy hitters

Pitfalls:

  • Does not tell you which items exist by itself
  • Counts are approximate
  • Can overestimate due to collisions
  • Not good when exact counts are required
  • Still needs non-trivial memory

Interview line:

Count-Min Sketch helps estimate frequencies in huge streams using bounded memory.

⬆ Back to Index

46. HyperLogLog¶

HyperLogLog (HLL) estimates the number of unique items in a dataset.

This is called cardinality estimation.

It answers:

How many distinct users/items/IPs did we see?

Instead of storing every unique item in a hash set, HLL uses a compact probabilistic representation.

Good for:

  • Billions of unique users/events
  • Limited memory
  • Approximate unique counts

⬆ Back to Index

47. HyperLogLog: How It Works¶

Basic intuition:

Rare hash patterns imply many unique items.

How it works:

  1. Hash each item
  2. Use part of hash to choose a bucket/register
  3. Use remaining bits to count leading zeros
  4. Track max leading-zero count per bucket
  5. Combine buckets to estimate cardinality

More buckets:

More memory
Better accuracy

HLL gives very memory-efficient approximate unique counts. ⬆ Back to Index

48. HyperLogLog: Use Cases and Pitfalls¶

Use HyperLogLog when:

  • You need distinct counts
  • Dataset is huge
  • Exact result is not required
  • Memory must stay small

Good use cases:

  • Daily active users
  • Monthly active users
  • Unique visitors per page
  • Unique IPs
  • Distinct search terms
  • Cache working-set size
  • Anti-scraping signals

Pitfalls:

  • Not exact
  • Not useful for small cardinalities
  • Cannot list the unique items
  • Do not use when exact counts are required

Interview line:

HLL is great for estimating unique counts at massive scale.

⬆ Back to Index

49. Approximate Quantiles / Bucket Algorithms¶

Approximate quantiles help answer:

What is p95 latency?
What is p99 response time?
What is median session duration?

Exact quantiles require storing/sorting all values, which is too expensive at scale.

Bucket/histogram approach:

  1. Define value ranges
  2. Count how many values fall into each range
  3. Estimate percentiles from cumulative counts

Example:

0–10ms     → 1,000
10–50ms    → 8,000
50–100ms   → 800
100–500ms  → 180
500ms+     → 20

This gives distribution insight without storing every measurement.

⬆ Back to Index

50. Bucket Algorithm Variants¶

Common variants:

Fixed-Width Buckets¶

0–10
10–20
20–30

Good when values are uniformly distributed and range is known.

Exponential Buckets¶

1–2ms
2–4ms
4–8ms
8–16ms

Good for wide ranges and latency metrics.

Dynamic Histograms¶

Buckets adjust based on incoming data distribution.

Pros:

  • Better accuracy for unknown distributions

Cons:

  • More complex
  • Rebalancing required

Important:

Quantile error is bounded by bucket width.

⬆ Back to Index

51. Approximate Quantiles: Use Cases and Pitfalls¶

Use bucket algorithms when:

  • Need percentiles/quantiles
  • Dataset is huge
  • Streaming analysis is needed
  • Bounded memory is required
  • Approximation is acceptable

Good use cases:

  • APM latency metrics
  • Infrastructure monitoring
  • p95/p99 response time
  • SLO compliance
  • A/B testing analytics
  • Autoscaling decisions

Pitfalls:

  • Poor bucket boundaries reduce accuracy
  • Extreme percentiles may be inaccurate
  • Values outside expected range need handling
  • Too-wide buckets lose precision

Interview line:

Histograms/buckets let me estimate percentiles with bounded memory.

⬆ Back to Index

52. Big Data Structures Decision Table¶

Problem Data Structure
Need membership check Bloom Filter
Need approximate frequency count Count-Min Sketch
Need unique count/cardinality HyperLogLog
Need percentiles/quantiles Histogram/Bucket Algorithm
Need exact membership Hash Set
Need exact counts Hash Map / Counter
Need exact distinct users Hash Set / DB query
Need p99 at huge scale Histogram / DDSketch-like approach

Simple rule:

Membership? → Bloom Filter
Frequency? → Count-Min Sketch
Distinct count? → HyperLogLog
Percentiles? → Buckets/Histograms

Use approximate structures only when exact structures are too expensive.

⬆ Back to Index

53. Final Combined Interview Answer¶

For high read traffic, I use caching first. I usually choose Redis with cache-aside and TTL, then add CDN for static/global content. I handle stampedes, hot keys, consistency, and cache failures using request coalescing, invalidation, replication, local fallback caches, and circuit breakers.

If one database cannot handle storage or read/write throughput, I consider sharding. I choose a shard key with high cardinality, even distribution, and alignment with query patterns. Hash-based sharding is a common default, while range and directory sharding are useful for specific cases. I also plan for hot spots, cross-shard queries, cross-shard transactions, and resharding.

To reduce data movement when nodes are added or removed, I use consistent hashing. Keys and servers are placed on a hash ring, and virtual nodes improve balance. Consistent hashing distributes keys, but hot traffic still needs replication, salting, or caching.

For massive datasets where exact data structures are too expensive, I use approximate big-data structures: Bloom Filters for membership, Count-Min Sketch for frequency, HyperLogLog for cardinality, and bucket/histogram algorithms for percentiles. I only use these when memory is constrained and approximation is acceptable.

⬆ Back to Index

54. Extra Code: Cache-Aside Pseudocode¶

⬆ Back to Index

In [ ]:
def get_user_profile(user_id, cache, db):
    cache_key = f"user:{user_id}:profile"

    cached = cache.get(cache_key)
    if cached:
        return cached

    profile = db.query(
        "SELECT id, name, email FROM users WHERE id = %s",
        [user_id]
    )

    cache.set(cache_key, profile, ttl_seconds=600)
    return profile

55. Extra Code: Hash-Based Shard Routing Pseudocode¶

⬆ Back to Index

In [ ]:
def get_shard_id(key, number_of_shards):
    return hash(key) % number_of_shards


def route_user_query(user_id, shards):
    shard_id = get_shard_id(user_id, len(shards))
    shard = shards[shard_id]

    return shard.query(
        "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s",
        [user_id]
    )

56. Extra Code: Simple Consistent Hash Ring Pseudocode¶

⬆ Back to Index

In [ ]:
import bisect
import hashlib

class ConsistentHashRing:
    def __init__(self, virtual_nodes=3):
        self.virtual_nodes = virtual_nodes
        self.ring = []
        self.node_map = {}

    def _hash(self, key):
        return int(hashlib.md5(key.encode()).hexdigest(), 16)

    def add_node(self, node):
        for i in range(self.virtual_nodes):
            virtual_key = f"{node}-vn{i}"
            position = self._hash(virtual_key)

            bisect.insort(self.ring, position)
            self.node_map[position] = node

    def remove_node(self, node):
        for i in range(self.virtual_nodes):
            virtual_key = f"{node}-vn{i}"
            position = self._hash(virtual_key)

            index = bisect.bisect_left(self.ring, position)
            if index < len(self.ring) and self.ring[index] == position:
                self.ring.pop(index)
                del self.node_map[position]

    def get_node(self, key):
        if not self.ring:
            return None

        position = self._hash(key)
        index = bisect.bisect_left(self.ring, position)

        if index == len(self.ring):
            index = 0

        return self.node_map[self.ring[index]]

57. Extra Code: Simple Bloom Filter Example¶

This is a simplified educational implementation.

⬆ Back to Index

In [ ]:
import hashlib

class SimpleBloomFilter:
    def __init__(self, size=1000, hash_count=3):
        self.size = size
        self.hash_count = hash_count
        self.bits = [0] * size

    def _hashes(self, item):
        item = str(item)
        for i in range(self.hash_count):
            data = f"{item}:{i}".encode()
            digest = hashlib.md5(data).hexdigest()
            yield int(digest, 16) % self.size

    def add(self, item):
        for position in self._hashes(item):
            self.bits[position] = 1

    def might_contain(self, item):
        return all(self.bits[position] == 1 for position in self._hashes(item))


bf = SimpleBloomFilter(size=100, hash_count=3)
bf.add("url-1")

print(bf.might_contain("url-1"))  # probably true
print(bf.might_contain("url-2"))  # definitely false if any bit is missing
